title: Number Base Converter
description: Convert between binary, octal, decimal, and hexadecimal instantly. Supports negative numbers and custom bases.

howToUse.label: Enter a number
howToUse.description: Type any integer in the input field. You can enter binary, octal, decimal, or hexadecimal values.

howToUse.label: Select the input base
howToUse.description: Choose the base of the number you entered (2, 8, 10, or 16).

howToUse.label: See all conversions
howToUse.description: The equivalent values in all other bases appear instantly below.

howToUse.label: Copy a result
howToUse.description: Click the copy button next to any result to copy it to your clipboard.

faq.question: What is a number base?
faq.answer: A number base (or radix) defines how many unique digits are used to represent numbers. Base 10 (decimal) uses 0–9; base 2 (binary) uses 0–1; base 16 (hexadecimal) uses 0–9 and A–F.

faq.question: Why do programmers use binary and hexadecimal?
faq.answer: Computers process data in binary (base 2) at the hardware level. Hexadecimal (base 16) is a compact shorthand — each hex digit represents exactly 4 binary bits, making it easy to read memory addresses, color codes, and byte values.

faq.question: What is octal (base 8) used for?
faq.answer: Octal is common in Unix/Linux file permission notation (e.g., chmod 755) and older computing systems. Each octal digit represents exactly 3 binary bits.

faq.question: Are negative numbers supported?
faq.answer: Yes. Prefix your number with a minus sign (−) and it will be converted correctly across all bases.

faq.question: What are the valid characters for hexadecimal?
faq.answer: Hexadecimal uses digits 0–9 and letters A–F (case-insensitive). For example, the decimal value 255 is FF in hexadecimal.
